speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Here. Pick this up. Think there'll be two places.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Scott, do you want to stand up? Just tell me if you do. I'm going to disconnect you.

words.text: do you want to stand up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I feel fine. I don't want to disturb this work of art.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's to your left. To your left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. We're interviewing Mr. George Scott. Can we go back now, and you are being caught and at the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, the car came, and they took us into this house. And we were there maybe two weeks in the house. I never asked for-- how come that we got caught, and he never, ever offered an explanation. In fact, there was very little conversation between us. Anyhow, we were brought food twice a day, and we were just waiting. We didn't know what was going to be. Then one day, we were told that we were being taken to another prison. And a policeman came and handcuffed us, and that felt really strange. And we went back to the same station where we got off the train some weeks earlier. And I remember standing at the station, and there was a group of civilians, three men and-- three women and a man. And they were talking from a distance, looking at us, pointing at us. And it felt awful to be handcuffed and even more awful it was that when I looked at them, I looked them straight in their face. I could see their eyes slide away and look away. And that was really hurtful because I didn't know why people would want to disown me. I-- I-- I haven't done anything bad or wrong. Anyhow, we were taken from prison to prison-- a few days here, a few days there. And at a larger station on our way to [INAUDIBLE]-- there was two of us-- [? Farcash ?] said he'd like to go to the washroom. And he bolted in a crowd. And I found out that he got back to Budapest, to the orphanage. Of course, I always did what I was told. And I never saw [? Farcash ?] again. But he did manage to get back to Budapest because when I got back myself, I talked to people, and they told me that, yeah, he did come back. And so we got to [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was a large camp, a precinct, perhaps a four-story red brick building in which, by the time I got there, there were hundreds, maybe 1,000 people. Some with kids, some with belongings. Some had suitcases. I had nothing. And we just lived there. We didn't work. We just waited. And we were barricaded. Nobody could move out. There were no countings. Like later on, we had to line up and to be counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us a reference for what year, what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was mid-summer, 1944. And around the tenth of August of '44. Later, I learned that this was the last transport, official transport, from Hungary into Birkenau. On the 10th of August, the bells were ringing, and we were asked to line up in front of the precinct. And I always wanted to escape, so I ran up on the top of-- it was a flat roof. I ran on top of the roof. And I could see the people shouting, calling to one another, lining up. And I was crouched behind a chimney. And the chimney covered me from the door leading up to the roof. And as I was there watching and observing, suddenly I heard the door open. And as the man looked at me, I looked at him, and we saw each other at the same time. And it was a man  off-- a young man, maybe 30, in a black leather jacket. Shiny black hair slunk onto his skull. And his face turned into a big grin, and I could feel the blood just-- just drain out of me. And I took off like a shot. I ran downstairs. And when the trials were run in Israel about Eichmann, I recognized that that face and that grin belonged to him. But at the time, I didn't how it was. I lined up. About midday we started-- everybody was accounted for. We started marching into the railway station of the-- of the city of [INAUDIBLE]. And we were marched not to the station, but one of the sides, along the station a bit, to one way. And there was a long line of red cattle cars with a center door, two small windows on each end, a sliding door in the middle. And they started-- as we lined up against the cars, they started loading us on the cars. And there were maybe 70, 80 people in a car. And when my turn came, I elbowed and slithered through people who were already in the wagon, and I found a place about the middle of the floor. I could see the window where I was, stooped down. But they still kept shoving people on. And people who came on first, who tried to get a corner for themselves or their friends or family, they were squeezed into smaller and smaller and smaller spaces, and they were protesting. And you know, I just wrote about this, the three days in the wagon, so I don't want to spend too much time on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell us anyway. I'd like-- you know, tell us about how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, OK. Well, it's fairly vivid in my mind because I was thinking about it. People tried to open their suitcases. Eventually, the door got slung shut, and I could hear the lock snap. And there were kids crying and mothers hushing their children, and some people moaning as if they were in pain. And I was just wondering what's-- what's-- what's coming off. And people were trying to open their cases, but there was no room-- enough room to open the case. And there was a general chaos of voices and shouts and talk. And then there was a shudder, and everybody fell against each other as the train started moving. And then I eventually got very hungry. And then there was nothing to do. And then the next day, the train stopped and we thought we were there. And we were wondering what's happening. And we could hear the wagon doors open. And [INAUDIBLE] was coming closer to our wagon. And then they shouted, 'toilet stop.' And not everyone-- some people got out, and we went to the other side of the embankment to relieve ourselves. And as I wrote in my little memories-- memoirs-- there was only the rain-starved grass and God and us, and the guards pointing guns at us. And we didn't know where we were, whether it was Poland or Austria or Hungary. Then we got back into the car. And at that time, you know, the general tenor of the sound level was quite low. The occasional whimper of a child. And then we got to Auschwitz. And we got out. And as we got out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get even to Auschwitz, what about food in the train? What  about to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- you know, the second day, hunger really bad, but the third day was overriding everything. And I-- I-- you know, I often-- and I must have fallen asleep. What I didn't tell you that after-- after a while, I wanted still to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a pocket knife, and I tried to make a hole where I was sitting. I could feel with my hand the little square that I could let myself go through. My hand got sore and bleeding, so I used the other hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this happened before the toilet rest. And at the toilet rest, I looked out and I saw that the locomotive was pushing the train, not pulling it. So I knew that that was not an avenue that I could utilize.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got in the train, and-- and-- and-- and the train kept moving. And people resigned themselves to what's next.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the third day, around midday, that we got to a place we didn't know where it was. And we got out of the train. And there were young men in blue and-- blue-striped clothes-- looked like pajamas-- telling us to move it. And there were, this time, German soldiers and dogs. And we were told to leave everything in the train. It'll be taken to our quarters where we are going to be working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this talk-- was this spoken in Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- German. German-- German, not Hungarian. But people who understood German, they told everybody else what was being said. A lot of Hungarian Jews spoke German. And we-- I heard expressions like [POLISH], which I didn't understand. It's Polish. It means fast, fast. Move it. And I remember scrambling out of there. I remember stretching. To the left of me was beautiful, lush, green grass on the other side of the fence, with gleaming, white-painted goal posts-- a soccer field. And then in front of us was this gate that said [GERMAN], which means 'Work will set you free.' And on the right side of me was barracks and more wires. And behind me-- I didn't know it then-- were the baths, the gas chambers, and the crematorium. And as the line kept drifting along, there was this German soldier. I could see him from a distance-- polished, black boots, immaculately dressed. Quite absorbed. White gloves. And he was telling people where to go. And, you know, my passion to look people in the eyes. I looked straight at him. I pulled myself up to my full height. And I could see him look at me for an instant. And he pointed, and I went. And about 60 of us, all men, were given-- we were shaved and shorn and sprayed and showered. And we were handed wooden shoes, those blue and white clothes, and we were marched into a camp, which we were told was the gypsy camp. There were no gypsies there. Apparently the gypsies were all killed the previous month, perhaps in July. This was mid-August, what I'm talking to you about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in block 9. And, of course, the inmates kept running, and asked us what's new? Where did you come from? What's happening? And they told us that this is-- this is not a good place. And they told us about the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the gypsy camp, to give you a visual description, there were the guard towers and a big gate as we walked through. And immediately next to it, I suppose, was the kitchen and where they kept food. And then there was two rows of blocks on each side of the road. And the even numbers on one side, the odd numbers on the other side. And about the middle of the camp, one of the buildings was a latrine with holes in it and water. You know, everybody says that this is controversial, but I have used it myself. That we were handed soaps that said RIF, [GERMAN] soap. It didn't form. It was kind of gunk. We didn't know what it was. So I guess the Germans hadn't left anything underutilized. Anyhow, we were put in this block and people kept coming. And I discovered that my grandmother's oldest brother and her younger brother were both in this camp. And the oldest brother was in charge of the disinfectant unit. And remarkably, this man-- his name was [? Shandar ?] [? Vol, ?]-- always referred to me as [INAUDIBLE] grandson-- never by my name. [INAUDIBLE], of course, was his sister. And I was the first grandson. So now I get a bit of reflected glory half a century later. [LAUGHS] Auschwitz was dreadful. It was very cold in the morning. At 5 o'clock in the morning, we had shouts of [GERMAN], which means coffee time. It was kind of a black slop that I-- I had acid stomach. I remember standing for hours spitting into a can because the saliva kept coming up. I still have a very poor stomach. It's a miracle that I survived. And I hope it's going to last for a while. Auschwitz-- after standing for hours-- they were called appels. We were counted five deep. People came and counted us. And then we were discharged. Then often, somebody started shouting 'oven' because the mornings were very cold. And we stood against one another for heat. And there we stood. And I remember once somebody decided, let's say the [INAUDIBLE]. We really said it out loud, everybody. And I remember the sky as grey as grey can be. And that was nothing. There were certainly no acknowledgements of any kind coming forth. And it's difficult for me, if not impossible-- and I would say the latter-- to assume that there is any kind of preordained higher power that would have helped Hitler to achieve all this. I think I very much see in everything that I have ever looked at, the machinations of man, not God, in any aspect of the world, past, present, or future. And at the same time, I must tell you that I do now and I have always believed in God. But it isn't a kind of God of hocus pocus, of books that-- everybody's got their own books to thump, and their own interpretation, which are, it seems to me, usually self-serving. And I do not wish to offend with this observation. And I do have respect for whatever people believe in, provided it does not hurt anyone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Auschwitz-- we're talking about Auschwitz. Yeah, looking back at Auschwitz, what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Birkenau, really. Auschwitz was a city, an industrial center. Usually when people talk about Auschwitz, they mean Birkenau. That was the death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau, if you did do something wrong, what would happen? Or what did you observe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to go back to the visual for a moment, there were camps on each side of us. If you were facing the trains as they were coming in, which was the center of this compound, to the left were a woman's camp. We could see bald ladies. We couldn't talk to them. The wires were electrified, and about a yard from the electric wire, there was a single wire running. And every now and again, there were guard towers where a guard was watching, presumably with guns. I have never noticed anybody being shot from there, but they were there, or at least the towers were there. I couldn't see whether somebody was inside or not. On the other side were the twin camps. They were called [GERMAN]. And they seemed to have been better fed than we were. I didn't see any experiments, but they were there, they were children, even younger than I was, and I was 14 at the time. I was even 14 when I was liberated. I wasn't 15 yet. Within three weeks I became 15. But I'm jumping the gun. At the very end of the camp was-- on one side, closer to the [GERMAN] camp, was a disinfectant unit. It was a kind of place where if you had a sore or a cut or anything, they could give you some sort of attention-- medical attention. And it is of this unit that my uncle was head of. And we had food once a day, and that was done by-- they handed a square-shaped, almost like a brick-shaped, bread-- very heavy-- to five of us. And it was handed out, and it maybe had some margarine or some honey or something with it. We each got one. And we very carefully-- that was a ritual-- cut it into equal pieces. And people took their part and they ate it. And that was it. That was once a day. The soup that was served-- I could never eat. I just couldn't eat it. And apart from that, there was coffee in the morning. That was the daily routine for food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember as we got there, we used to get together, and for hours we used to recall how our mothers or grandmothers or aunts baked, and the smells and the whipped cream and the butter and the meat. And there we sat, salivating like crazy and remembering all the good flavors. But after about a month, this stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people went into themselves more and more. You seemed to somehow-- you're sucked up by your own being. And you're cognizant of people around you, and they're all shadows, dragging themselves, breathing like yourself. But there is very little interaction, what you would call, except lining up for appels, et cetera. And one thing that, of course, I cannot forget-- the first selection inside the gypsy camp came on Rosh Hashanah of 1944. And that was-- I don't think it was by accident. And I was selected to-- because by then, I was very skinny. And Mengele pointed one way, and I saw that all the skinny people went in one group and the fat ones, or the better, not as skinny, on the other. And behind him, I scooted into the other group. And I manage to get away with it. Somebody else did it minutes after me, and he was caught and thrown back in the other group. And these people were locked up in a block for a day or two, and then that was a [GERMAN]. Bells ringing, and that meant nobody was allowed to come out. And when it ended, that block was emptied. Trucks came-- you could hear trucks coming-- and they were gone. And when this same thing happened two weeks later, many of the people had a skin condition. And somehow or other, Mengele was convinced to give them another chance. And I was in that group, unfortunately. And in that group, I got caught. And they locked us up in one of these blocks. And there was a boy with me whose brother was still on the other side. His name was [? Vol ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And he was from [INAUDIBLE], the same village as my grandmother's family came from, my grandmother's parents. And strangely enough, his name was also [? Vol, ?] but no relationship. And I remember, suddenly the door opened, and there was a young man throwing his arms and screaming. And that was my grandmother's brother saying, George, come and run. And I took up and I went like a shot. And then the following morning, that whole block was emptied. But as I was running out, I remember this boy shouting after me, 'Tell my brother to get my ration.' Because in this place, we didn't get anything. And that felt like, you know, what a rip off, because we were singled out for-- what do you call-- unusual punishment. But one [INAUDIBLE] I cannot understand that, or certainly find it hurts one's sense of justice or fairness. It's really ludicrous at this point for even-- to remember it. But nonetheless, these feelings were very real. After this incident, every night we could hear the guns in Auschwitz. And we were hoping that the war would be over, but it wasn't. And after this incident, the very first opportunity I managed to get, I got into a labor group. They were calling for people. Myself, and as I found later, three other kids got into this group. And we were handed a bread, we were handed an overcoat with the back cut out. It was a grey overcoat, a civilian coat, but the entire back was a big square was cut out with the letters K Z sewn in on a thin piece of cloth, which overcoat, incidentally, I had for many, many months, almost, until I was liberated in Dachau. And we got on this train, and we ended up in a place called Kaufering. It was called [INAUDIBLE], Kaufering. It was close to-- close to Munich-- about 60 miles from Munich. And again, we had to get undressed, And everybody got examined. And lo and behold, four kids-- I was one of them-- they wanted to send us back. We were not working material. And the German commandant intervened and said that he'll put us in the kitchen to peel potatoes. Imagine that. And a fellow called [? Bill ?] [? Gleed, ?] who incidentally lives in Toronto, and who I met and who has pictures of the camp because he went back and took pictures, and myself went into the SS kitchen. And I was there about two months peeling potatoes. This was mid-November, so I was there till early January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was given a number. I was [GERMAN], which is 116,288. It was a Dachau number. And I was registered as George Spiegel. Another thing I didn't tell you about Auschwitz-- an interesting thing. As soon as we got in there, we were handed [? postcards ?] that we've got jobs. It was pre-stamped. We had to address. We're doing well. And we had to send that back to our relatives. And my aunt Bertha, who's the only person of my six sisters or my brothers who survived, got this postcard and momentarily, her fears were allayed about her parents, the kids, myself. This is really an aside which I should've mentioned at the beginning. Anyhow, in January, we were taken out of the kitchen because some Hungarian women marched from Hungary. They were now walking. Arrived-- Jewish women-- and they were put into the kitchen. And within about a week, I was transferred to another camp called [INAUDIBLE], and that was in Landsberg, again, in the same district.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get there, did anybody try to escape from Auschwitz or Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that would have been impossible. No language, no strength, no-- I find that practically inconceivable for anyone to escape from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to rebel?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no life force. There was-- there was-- One thing I do remember in Auschwitz that the [INAUDIBLE] Rabbi's son, full of sores-- one of the sons, a tall young boy, covered with sores-- he wouldn't eat. I remember him being taken away, put on one of the trucks. This was in October of 1944. Because I know he was somebody because the other people deferred to him. And he was just a young boy, maybe a little older. He must've been 16. I was 14. Tall, but covered with sores, skin and bones. You know, no one can knock the power of prayer. It really does take a hold on people. And it is-- I don't know that I am insecure about God-- I mean, the conventional God-- because I'm Jewish so I was born insecure, or whether it is something that goes beyond my inheritance. This boy was very sure of God. And even though I know that religious people say it's a matter of saving your life, you can eat non-kosher things. He didn't. And I'm sure that he was gassed and burned. In this [INAUDIBLE] in Landsberg, we worked in underground facilities. We don't know what it was. I had to carry pebbles in a wheelbarrow from one place to another. I really didn't understand what I was doing. But there were civilian Germans in charge of us. And occasionally, some of them gave me a bit of extra soup. And I could feel that they were kindly. And there were others, especially younger ones, who insisted that we march in sync to work, and we brought back people who died from the job. We pushed them back. And we were counted out and we were counted in. And about-- the end of February, early March, I was sick. And I was put into what they call a [INAUDIBLE] camp. And it was another place where we were living in huts covered with earth. And there, because of my experience, I was put into the kitchen, but for a very short time because my friend [INAUDIBLE] was there from the village [INAUDIBLE]. And I stole potatoes for him in the-- I tied my pants and I put them in where my trousers were. And I got caught and I lost my job. And I understand that he had survived and he's in Israel somewhere. His name is [INAUDIBLE]. And he would remember this story. Anyhow, as the time grew on, they put us on-- one day, they put us on trains. [INAUDIBLE] thinks we're relaxed. This was April, mid- April. And we were on the road for a long time-- maybe 10 days. I remember a military train stood next to us and it got machine-gunned. And as we were walking, I saw a hand-- just nothing, only a hand in the snow. You know, through that fog of being numb, it still-- it felt bizarre. And then we got into a place we didn't know where it was at night. And in the morning, the Americans walked in. And I am there waving to them. And my picture was, to my amazement, in the paper five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop there now and continue.
